mapQuestions.question: Which part of the world is the UAE in?
mapQuestions.answer: The Middle East. The UAE sits on the south-east corner of the Arabian Peninsula, on the Persian Gulf 🌏

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of the UAE?
mapQuestions.answer: Abu Dhabi. Most people guess Dubai, but Dubai is just the biggest city. Abu Dhabi is the capital and is also the biggest emirate by area.

mapQuestions.question: How many emirates make up the UAE?
mapQuestions.answer: Seven: Abu Dhabi, Dubai, Sharjah, Ajman, Umm al-Quwain, Ras al-Khaimah, and Fujairah. They joined to make one country in 1971.

mapQuestions.question: What two countries border the UAE?
mapQuestions.answer: Saudi Arabia to the west and south, and Oman to the east. Just across the Gulf to the north is Iran.

mapQuestions.question: How much of the UAE is desert?
mapQuestions.answer: Around 80%. The Empty Quarter (Rub' al Khali) on the southern edge is the largest continuous sand desert on Earth.

foods.name: Shawarma
foods.description: Marinated lamb or chicken sliced off a slowly turning vertical spit, stuffed into pita bread with garlic sauce, pickles, and sometimes fries inside.

foods.name: Mixed grill (mishwi)
foods.description: A platter of kebabs — lamb shish, chicken tikka, kofta (spiced minced meat) — grilled over charcoal, served with rice, hummus, salads and warm flatbreads.

foods.name: Khubz + hummus
foods.description: Soft round flatbread torn into pieces and used to scoop hummus (mashed chickpeas with tahini and lemon), baba ganoush (smoky aubergine), and labneh (strained yogurt).

foods.name: Luqaimat
foods.description: Tiny crispy fried dough balls drizzled with sweet date syrup and sometimes sesame seeds. A Ramadan favourite, eaten as a sweet snack year-round.

foods.name: Karak chai
foods.description: Strong black tea boiled with cardamom, sweet evaporated milk and a lot of sugar. Sold from drive-through windows in Dubai for the equivalent of about 50p a cup.

foods.name: Fresh dates
foods.description: Dates are eaten constantly here, especially with bitter Arabic coffee. Some date varieties (medjool, khalas, ajwa) are eaten fresh, soft and sticky-sweet.

scavengerItems.label: The Burj Khalifa, or any building taller than 50 floors
A real camel (in the desert, on a roadside, or in a race)
A man wearing a white kandura (long Emirati robe) and headscarf
A row of date palms loaded with dates or fronds
A mosque with a tall minaret and a turquoise or white dome
A souk (traditional market) selling gold, spices or fabrics
A man-made island shaped like a palm tree (or a globe)
An "Abra" wooden boat crossing Dubai Creek with passengers
A 50°C dashboard temperature on a summer afternoon (or any wild heat reading)
A real falcon (in a souk, on a wrist, or at a falconry centre)
A Bedouin tent set up in a desert camp
An Arabic-script sign with the English translation underneath

geoMatches.description: The biggest city. Tallest building in the world (Burj Khalifa, 828m), giant indoor ski slope, sail-shaped 7-star hotel, palm-tree-shaped islands.
The capital, with the huge white Sheikh Zayed Grand Mosque (one of the largest mosques on Earth), the Louvre Abu Dhabi, and the Yas Island F1 racetrack.
The "Garden City" inland. A real desert oasis with 50,000+ date palms, falaj (ancient water channels), and a UNESCO-protected oasis right in town.
The world's largest continuous sand desert, stretching across the south. Towering orange dunes, complete silence, and almost no people. The set of "Lawrence of Arabia".
A mountain enclave east of Dubai. Jagged Hajar Mountains, a turquoise lake good for kayaking, and cooler weather than the city.
The "cultural emirate". Museums, calligraphy, the Sharjah Art Foundation, and stricter Islamic rules (no alcohol). Right next door to Dubai but very different in feel.

senses.smell: Frankincense and oud in shops, shisha smoke, rosewater drizzled on dates, fresh hot bread from a tandoor, desert dust mixed with sea salt, cold marble malls full of perfume…
senses.hear: The call to prayer five times a day, Arabic and English mixed in the same sentence, "Habibi!", car horns, fountain music outside Dubai Mall, helicopters, falcons screeching…
senses.taste: Sweet sticky dates, salty hummus, sweet milky karak tea, fresh shawarma juice, sharp tabbouleh, ice-cold rosewater drink, dark Arabic coffee with cardamom…
senses.feel: 45°C heat in summer (avoid noon), blasting cold air-con inside every building, soft sand between your toes, cool marble mall floors, fine desert dust on your skin, sea breeze at sunset…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: How did Dubai go from a fishing village to skyscraper city in 50 years?
stories.body: In 1960 Dubai was a small village of pearl divers and fishermen, with a population of about 40,000 and almost no paved roads. Today it has 3.6 million people and one of the most futuristic skylines on Earth. The reason: oil was found in 1966. Dubai used the oil money to build airports, ports and free-trade zones, and then made itself the trade and tourism hub of the Middle East. The Burj Khalifa, currently the tallest building in the world at 828 metres, was built in 6 years and opened in 2010. Its elevators are the fastest in the world. From the top, you can watch the sun set, race down to the ground floor, and watch the sun set AGAIN at sea level.

stories.question: How can camels survive without water for a week?
stories.body: Camels have been used for transport, milk, meat and racing across the Arabian Peninsula for thousands of years. A camel can drink 100 litres of water in 13 minutes, then go a week or more without drinking again. Their humps store fat (not water — that's a common myth) which the body breaks down for energy and water when needed. Their thick lashes keep sand out of their eyes. Their feet are wide and padded for walking on hot sand. Their body temperature can rise and fall 6°C between night and day without harming them — most mammals would die. Camel races are still huge in the UAE, with robot jockeys riding them instead of children (human child jockeys were banned).

stories.question: Why do you hear the call to prayer five times a day?
stories.body: Most Emiratis are Muslim. Practising Muslims pray five times every day — at dawn, noon, mid-afternoon, sunset, and after dark. From every mosque, an Imam sings the "adhan", the call to prayer, over loudspeakers, so the whole neighbourhood can hear. The first one of the day is around 4:30am, the last around 8pm. Mosques are everywhere, often beautifully tiled, with tall thin minaret towers. In some shops, staff briefly close and pray on a small mat behind the till before reopening. Non-Muslims aren't expected to follow the rules, but should be respectful — especially during Ramadan, when Muslims fast from sunrise to sunset for a whole month, and eating in public during daylight is considered rude.

stories.question: Who built modern Dubai?
stories.body: Around 88% of people living in the UAE are foreigners, not Emiratis. The vast majority of the workers who actually built Dubai's skyscrapers, malls and roads come from India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, the Philippines, Nepal and Sri Lanka. Many are construction workers and domestic workers who send money home to their families. They make up the largest single group in every Emirate. The UAE's "kafala" sponsorship system has been criticised internationally for tying workers to single employers and making it hard for them to leave. The country has reformed some rules in recent years, but conditions for the lowest-paid workers are still often very tough, especially in summer when temperatures hit 50°C on building sites.
stories.thinkingQuestion: A small country with mostly foreign workers can build wonders. But what does it owe the people who actually do the building?

stories.question: What kind of country is the UAE?
stories.body: The UAE is ruled by seven hereditary sheikhs (royal families), one from each emirate. Together they choose a president from among themselves — by tradition, the ruler of Abu Dhabi. It is not a democracy: there are no national elections, no political parties, and strict limits on free speech and protest. But the country has invested its oil wealth in modern infrastructure, free education, universal healthcare, very low crime, and even a UAE Space Agency that has successfully landed a probe on Mars. It is also still working on big rights issues — workers' rights, women's rights, LGBTQ+ rights — and on these the UAE is in a different place to many Western countries. It is one of the safest, cleanest, most futuristic countries you can visit, sitting next to neighbours dealing with civil war and instability.

convoQuestions.question: Best Emirati / Middle Eastern food you have eaten?
Have you been to the top of a really tall building yet? Was it scary?
How does the heat feel compared to back home?
Have you heard the call to prayer? Where were you when it started?
Old (camels, desert, souks) vs new (skyscrapers, ski slopes, malls) — which side has surprised you more?
88% of the people here are foreign workers. Does the city feel international or Emirati to you?

wordSearch.hint: Emirati foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Dromedary camel
animals.description: Beside every desert road and at the camel-race tracks. Robots ride them now (no more child jockeys).

animals.name: Greater flamingo
animals.description: On the Ras Al Khor lagoon right next to Dubai. Pink flock against skyscrapers — surreal.

animals.name: Geckos and skinks
animals.description: On every wall in the cooler hours. Tiny, fast, harmless.

animals.name: Arabian Mau
animals.description: Short-haired street cat native to the Arabian Peninsula. Often spotted in old souks.

animals.name: Saker falcon
animals.description: Falconry is the UAE national sport. Look in souks and at falcon hospitals.

animals.name: Arabian oryx
animals.description: Once extinct in the wild, reintroduced — white antelope with long straight horns in desert reserves.
